content.decision_question: Given contested evidence on whether selective-track attendance causally benefits disadvantaged students beyond family-background effects, should quota-based access expansion proceed on equity-access grounds alone, or should resources instead target interventions (e.g., earlier, kindergarten-stage support) with a more established evidence base for durable benefit?
content.evidence_boundary: Further causal (e.g., quasi-experimental or longitudinal) evidence can help resolve whether track attendance itself, independent of composition, confers durable benefits — this is an answerable empirical open question. However, no evidence can resolve whether equal access to prestigious institutions has independent value as a matter of fairness even absent proven durable benefit; that is a separate normative question about the meaning of equal opportunity.
content.tension: Targeted quotas and outreach (T5) aim to change who is admitted to selective tracks without altering the track structure, framed as an equity-enhancing compositional fix. Yet evidence suggests that value-added gains attributed to selective tracks may substantially reflect family background rather than institutional effect, and at least one study finds no measurable tertiary-education benefit from track selection. This raises a genuine empirical uncertainty about whether the 'prize' being redistributed via quotas actually confers durable benefit, which is distinct from — but entangled with — the value question of whether equal access to prestige institutions is intrinsically valuable regardless of measured long-run effect.
content.title: Preserving track structure while redistributing access: contested benefit of the prize being redistributed
value_poles: equal access to prestigious institutions as an equity goal in itself
ensuring interventions produce genuine, durable benefit rather than symbolic inclusion
